meta.title: Pain Management Clinic Software | RTM Billing, Patient Flow & Prior Auth | clinIQ
meta.description: $12K/month in RTM revenue. 56 minutes recovered per procedure day. Prior auth tracking with LOMN templates. clinIQ for interventional pain practices.

hero.h1: $12,000/month in RTM revenue. 56 minutes recovered per procedure day.
hero.subhead: Your chronic pain patients are already reporting symptoms between visits. Your procedure room turnover is already eating margin. Your staff is already writing letters of medical necessity. clinIQ turns all of that into revenue, capacity, and time.

sections.h2: RTM revenue: $120/patient/month you're not billing
sections.content:
Your chronic pain patients already track pain levels between visits. They report functional status. They tell you when medications aren't working.

That's Remote Therapeutic Monitoring. CPT 98980 and 98981. Billable.

clinIQ RTM does this:

• Patient app for pain scores, medication adherence, and functional status — no hardware required
• Automatic time tracking toward the 20-minute billing threshold
• Documentation that meets payer requirements — clean claims, fewer denials
• Dashboard showing enrolled patients, billing status, and revenue by provider

The math: 100 chronic pain patients × $120/month = $12,000/month.

That's not new clinical work. Your physicians already review patient check-ins and adjust treatment. clinIQ captures it as billable RTM.

sections.h2: Procedure room turnover: 56 minutes recovered per day
sections.content:
You've got 8 injection slots from 7am to noon. Every minute of turnover between cases is margin you're not recovering.

• 8 procedures × 15-minute turnover = 2 hours of dead time
• 8 procedures × 8-minute turnover = 64 minutes of dead time
• Difference: 56 minutes = one more injection = $800-1,500

clinIQ Patient Flow does this:

• Real-time room status: occupied, turning over, ready
• Pre-op checklist visibility: Is the next patient checked in? Consented? IV placed?
• Turnover timer: Starts automatically when patient leaves, stops when next patient enters
• Bottleneck alerts: 'Room 2 ready, next patient not in pre-op' — intervene before you lose the slot

Your MA isn't walking the hall asking 'is the room ready?' Your provider isn't waiting on information. Everyone sees the same board.

sections.h2: Prior auth + LOMN: Templates that write themselves
sections.content:
Another epidural denied. Another letter of medical necessity to write. Another week your patient waits in pain.

clinIQ Pre-Auth does this:

• Status tracking: Pending, approved, denied, appealed — for every patient, every procedure
• Expiration alerts: 'Auth expires in 72 hours, patient not yet scheduled' — before you lose it
• LOMN templates: Pre-built for epidurals, facet injections, RFA, trigger points — auto-populated from patient chart
• Appeal workflow: Denial reason captured, peer-to-peer scheduled, outcome logged

Your staff stops calling payers to check status. The status is in the system. When something needs action, it surfaces.

LOMN generation: Select procedure, select patient, generate letter. Conservative care history, imaging findings, failed treatments — pulled from the chart. Edit, sign, submit. Five minutes instead of thirty.

sections.h2: Patient flow: Everyone sees who's next
sections.content:
Chronic pain visits aren't 15 minutes. They're vitals, UDS collection, MA interview, provider assessment, PDMP check, education, checkout. Thirty to forty-five minutes that your schedule calls a 'follow-up.'

clinIQ Patient Flow does this:

• Stage tracking: Check-in → Vitals → UDS → With MA → With Provider → Procedure → Recovery → Checkout
• Wait time visibility: Patient checked in at 10:15, still in vitals at 10:32 — alert before they complain
• Provider queue: Who's ready for me right now? How many waiting? No walking to the front desk to ask
• MA queue: Which patients need me? Who's been waiting longest? Which rooms are open?

Nobody asks 'who's next?' because everyone can see it.

sections.h2: Compliance workflows built into patient flow
sections.content:
PDMP checks. Treatment agreements. Urine drug screens. Pill counts. Your physicians spend more time on documentation than treating pain.

clinIQ doesn't replace your PDMP portal — that's state-mandated. But it tracks that the check happened:

• UDS status visible at each stage: collected, resulted, reviewed
• Compliance checklist per patient: treatment agreement signed, PDMP checked, pill count completed
• Alert if compliance step is missing before provider encounter

Nothing gets missed. Nothing falls through the cracks. The audit trail builds itself.

sections.h2: Live in two weeks, not six months
sections.content:
Week 1: We map your workflow — clinic days, procedure days, injection observation. We configure clinIQ to match how you actually work.

Week 2: Staff training. Front desk learns check-in. MAs learn the queue. Providers learn their dashboard. Most staff are comfortable in an hour.

Week 3: Go live with support. We're watching, you're asking questions, we're adjusting.

Week 4: You're running. Turnover times are tracked. RTM is billing. Prior auths have visibility.

No EHR replacement. No workflow redesign consultants. No six-month implementation.

workflow.note: Clinic days and procedure days run different workflows. clinIQ handles both.

pricing.starter: $249/month — Check-in, patient flow, basic analytics. One location.
pricing.professional: $499/month — Everything. RTM billing, prior auth tracking, multi-location, priority support.
pricing.implementation: $750 one-time — Workflow mapping, configuration, training, go-live support.
pricing.integration: Custom quote — eClinicalWorks, Athena, and other EHR integrations.

faqs.q: How much RTM revenue can we actually capture?
faqs.a: Most pain practices see $100-150 per enrolled patient per month. 100 patients = $10,000-15,000/month. The work is already happening — clinIQ captures it as billable.

faqs.q: Does clinIQ generate letters of medical necessity?
faqs.a: Yes. LOMN templates for common pain procedures auto-populate from patient charts. Conservative care history, imaging, failed treatments — pulled automatically. Edit, sign, submit.

faqs.q: How do you track prior auth status?
faqs.a: Every auth request tracked from submission to decision. Pending, info requested, approved, denied, appealed. Expiration alerts before you lose the auth. No more calling to check status.

faqs.q: Does this replace our EHR?
faqs.a: No. clinIQ is an operations layer. Your EHR handles charts. We handle patient flow, RTM, and prior auth visibility. We integrate — we don't replace.

faqs.q: How long until we see ROI?
faqs.a: Most practices see ROI in month one. One recovered procedure slot per week pays for Professional. RTM billing on 50 patients covers it twice over.

cta.subhead: 15-minute demo. We'll show you RTM revenue, procedure room recovery, and prior auth tracking configured for pain management.

meta.title: Addiction Medicine & MAT Clinic Software | RTM Billing & Patient Flow | clinIQ
meta.description: $7,500/month in behavioral health RTM. Zero missed Sublocade injections. Real-time visibility from intake to observation. clinIQ for MAT clinics.

hero.h1: $7,500/month in RTM revenue. Zero missed injection appointments.
hero.subhead: Your MAT patients already check in between visits. Your Sublocade schedule already drives revenue. Your observation chairs already bottleneck flow. clinIQ turns monitoring into billing, scheduling into revenue capture, and chaos into visibility.

sections.h2: Behavioral health RTM: $100/patient/month
sections.content:
Your patients already report between visits. Cravings. Mood. Triggers. Medication adherence. That's therapeutic monitoring — and it's billable.

CPT 98980 and 98981. Remote Therapeutic Monitoring for behavioral health.

clinIQ RTM does this:

• Patient app for symptom tracking — cravings, mood, triggers, medication adherence
• Automatic time tracking toward the 20-minute billing threshold
• Counselor dashboard: Which patients checked in? Who needs follow-up? Who's at risk?
• Documentation that meets payer requirements for behavioral health RTM

The math: 75 MAT patients × $100/month = $7,500/month.

Your counselors are already reviewing patient check-ins. clinIQ captures it as billable RTM.

sections.h2: Injection scheduling: $1,800-2,200 per Sublocade you don't miss
sections.content:
Sublocade is monthly. Vivitrol is monthly. But insurance authorizes on different cycles. Patients miss appointments. Your injection coordinator tracks this in spreadsheets.

One missed injection = $1,800-2,200 in lost revenue. One expired auth = a patient at risk.

clinIQ Scheduling does this:

• Injection pipeline: Every patient's due date, auth status, inventory match — one view
• Auth expiration alerts: 'Sublocade auth expires in 5 days, patient not scheduled'
• Waitlist fill: Cancellation at 8am? Waitlist patient gets texted. Slot filled by 9am.
• Inventory sync: Injections in the fridge matched to scheduled patients

No spreadsheets. No memory. No missed revenue.

sections.h2: Observation chair visibility: No more bottlenecks
sections.content:
Post-injection observation is 30 minutes minimum. You have 4 observation chairs. You scheduled 6 injection patients today.

If injections start late, observation runs late. If observation runs late, your afternoon backs up.

clinIQ Patient Flow does this:

• Observation timer: Started 10:15, ends 10:45, chair available 10:47
• Chair status: Occupied (18 min remaining), Available, Turning over
• Injection room ready alert: 'Observation chair freeing in 5 min — prep next patient'
• Bottleneck prevention: See the backup coming, not after it happens

Your injection coordinator sees the entire pipeline. No walking the hall. No guessing.

sections.h2: Intake to checkout: Every stage visible
sections.content:
MAT patients cycle through intake, urine collection, vitals, provider, injection, observation. Your front desk juggles benefit verification and crisis interruptions. Your MAs context-switch constantly.

clinIQ Patient Flow does this:

• Stage tracking: Check-in → Intake → UDS → Vitals → With MA → With Provider → Injection → Observation → Checkout
• UDS status: Collected, resulted, reviewed — visible at every stage
• Crisis flag: Patient in crisis? Flag visible to clinical staff immediately
• Queue by role: MAs see their patients. Providers see theirs. Nobody asks 'who's next?'

The chaos doesn't disappear. It becomes visible. And visible chaos is manageable chaos.

sections.h2: Private check-in: Dignity from the door
sections.content:
Your patients don't want to announce 'I'm here for Suboxone' at a shared desk. They don't want to sign a paper log everyone can see.

clinIQ Check-In does this:

• Tablet check-in: Private corner, no verbal announcement required
• Phone check-in: QR code from the parking lot — in the queue before they walk in
• Queue numbers: Display shows numbers, never names
• Reason for visit: Never displayed, never announced

Privacy isn't a feature. It's the baseline for addiction medicine.

sections.h2: Configured for MAT, not adapted from primary care
sections.content:
MAT clinics aren't family medicine. Your workflow is different. Your patient population is different. We don't hand you a template designed for something else.

Week 1: We map your flow — intake, UDS, vitals, provider, injection types, observation protocols. Group sessions if you have them.

Week 2: Training by role. Front desk, MAs, counselors, providers, injection coordinator.

Week 3: Go live. Observation timers running. Injection pipeline visible. RTM capturing time.

Week 4: You're running. Revenue is billing. Flow is visible. Staff isn't drowning.

workflow.note: Not every patient hits every stage. Refills skip injection. Crisis visits skip the line. The system handles all of it.

faqs.q: Is behavioral health RTM actually billable?
faqs.a: Yes. CPT 98980 and 98981 apply to behavioral health monitoring. Patient-reported symptoms, counselor review, treatment adjustments — that's RTM. Most payers cover it.

faqs.q: How do you track injection schedules?
faqs.a: Each patient's due date, auth status, and inventory match visible in one view. Alerts when auths are expiring. Waitlist fills cancellations automatically.

faqs.q: Can patients check in privately?
faqs.a: Yes. Tablet in a private area or phone check-in from the parking lot. Queue numbers on display, never names. No announcing why they're here.

faqs.q: How do you handle observation timing?
faqs.a: Timer starts when injection is administered. Observation end time visible. Chair availability predicted. Next injection can be prepped before the chair is even free.

faqs.a: We configure for your model. OBOT in primary care settings is simpler. Traditional OTP has more stages. Same system, different configuration.

meta.title: Urgent Care Patient Flow Software | Reduce LWBS, Increase Throughput | clinIQ
meta.description: 15-20% LWBS reduction. 12 minutes off average wait time. Real-time door-to-door visibility. clinIQ for urgent care centers.

hero.h1: Every LWBS patient is $150-300 walking out the door.
hero.subhead: Your LWBS rate is 5-8%. That's 3-5 patients per day who checked in, sat down, and left. At $200 average, that's $600-1,000/day in lost revenue. clinIQ shows you who's about to leave — before they do.

sections.h2: LWBS recovery: $18,000-30,000/month you're losing
sections.content:
Industry average LWBS is 3-5%. Busy days hit 8-10%. You're not tracking it because you can't — your system only knows when patients were seen, not when they left.

clinIQ tracks LWBS in real-time:

• Walkout detection: Patient marked 'departed' without being seen — captured immediately
• Wait time alerts: Patient waiting 25+ minutes? Alert fires. Intervene before they leave.
• LWBS dashboard: Daily, weekly, monthly rates. By hour, by day of week, by provider on duty.
• Recovery opportunity: 5% LWBS at 60 patients/day = 3 patients = $600/day = $18,000/month

You can't recover patients you don't know you're losing. clinIQ shows you the leak.

sections.h2: Door-to-door time: 12 minutes faster on average
sections.content:
Door-to-door is the only metric. But you're not measuring it — you're guessing based on how the day felt.

clinIQ measures every stage:

• Arrival to triage: 3 minutes
• Triage to room: 18 minutes (here's your bottleneck)
• Room to provider: 4 minutes
• Provider to discharge: 12 minutes

Now you know where time goes. Triage to room is the problem? Add a room. Provider to discharge? Streamline paperwork.

Clinics using clinIQ see 10-15 minute reductions in average door-to-door time. At 60 patients/day, that's capacity for 5-8 more patients.

sections.h2: LobbyView: Patients stop asking 'how much longer?'
sections.content:
Patients don't leave because of wait time. They leave because of uncertainty. 45 minutes with information is tolerable. 20 minutes with no information is intolerable.

clinIQ LobbyView does this:

• Queue position display: 'Your number: 12. Position: 4th. Estimated wait: 18 minutes.'
• HIPAA-compliant: Numbers only, never names, never conditions
• Real-time updates: Position changes as patients are seen
• Multi-screen: Waiting room, triage area, provider area — different views for different roles

Your front desk stops fielding 'how much longer?' every 5 minutes. Patients can see. They can decide to wait or leave based on real information, not frustration.

sections.h2: Real-time patient board: No more whiteboard fiction
sections.content:
Your whiteboard was accurate two hours ago. Room 3 says 'chest pain' but that patient went to the ED an hour ago. Nobody updated it.

clinIQ Patient Flow does this:

• Auto-updating status: Patient checks in → status updates. Patient rooms → status updates. No manual maintenance.
• Room status: Occupied, available, needs turnover — at a glance
• Triage integration: ESI level assigned, priority visible, reassessment alerts for long waits
• Provider view: Who's ready in which room? No walking the hall to find out.

Digital patient status that maintains itself. Fiction becomes fact.

sections.h2: Volume spikes: See them at noon, not at 3pm
sections.content:
Monday morning: 3 patients. Monday afternoon: 27 patients. You can't predict it. But you can see it happening.

clinIQ Analytics does this:

• Real-time queue depth: 4 waiting, 6 in rooms, 2 in discharge
• Trend alerts: 'Queue depth increasing — 8 patients arrived in last 30 minutes'
• Historical patterns: Mondays spike at 1pm, Saturdays peak at 11am
• Staffing insights: When do you need the extra provider? Data, not guessing.

You see the surge at 12:30. You pull someone to help at 12:35. You prevent the 2pm meltdown.

sections.h2: Live in one week, running smooth in two
sections.content:
Urgent care doesn't have time for six-month implementations.

Day 1-2: We map your flow. Registration, triage, rooming, provider, discharge.

Day 3-4: Staff training. Front desk, triage, providers. Each role sees their view.

Day 5: Go live. We're monitoring. You're using it. We're adjusting.

Week 2: You're running independently. Patient flow is visible. LWBS is tracked. Door-to-door is measured.

No disruption. No consultants. No lengthy implementation.

workflow.note: Simple visits skip pending results. Walk-outs captured when they happen, not discovered later.

faqs.q: How do you calculate LWBS rate?
faqs.a: Patients who check in but depart without being seen. Timestamped automatically. Daily/weekly/monthly rates by hour, day, provider. No manual tracking.

faqs.q: Can we show wait times on our website?
faqs.a: Yes. Current estimated wait time can feed to your website, Google listing, or external signage. Patients know before they arrive.

faqs.q: How accurate are the wait time estimates?
faqs.a: Based on actual current queue depth and historical throughput. Updates in real-time as patients move through stages. More accurate than guessing.

faqs.q: Does this work for multi-site groups?
faqs.a: Yes. Multi-location dashboard compares wait times, LWBS, throughput across all sites. Regional managers see everything. Site staff sees their location.

faqs.q: What about walk-ins vs online check-ins?
faqs.a: Both enter the same queue. Online check-ins get a head start on paperwork. Same visibility, same flow.

meta.title: Physical Therapy Software | RTM for Home Exercise & Multi-Patient Flow | clinIQ
meta.description: $10,400/month in musculoskeletal RTM. Zero patients forgotten in warm-up. Insurance cap visibility at check-in. clinIQ for PT practices.

hero.h1: $10,400/month in RTM. Zero patients waiting unnoticed.
hero.subhead: Your home exercise programs are therapeutic monitoring — billable as RTM. Your therapists juggle 4 patients across 3 zones — now with visibility. Your insurance cap surprises — eliminated at check-in.

sections.h2: Musculoskeletal RTM: $130/patient/month for HEP monitoring
sections.content:
Your patients go home with exercises. Stretches, strengthening, mobility work. Are they doing them? You ask. They say yes. You don't actually know until they're not progressing.

That home exercise compliance is billable. CPT 98977 (musculoskeletal RTM), 98980, 98981.

clinIQ RTM does this:

• Patient app: Exercise completion logging, pain scores, functional status — simple, no barriers
• Compliance dashboard: Who's doing their HEP? Who's not? Who needs a call?
• Automatic time tracking: Every review counts toward the 20-minute billing threshold
• Pre-visit insight: You know if they've been compliant before they walk in

The math: 80 patients × $130/month = $10,400/month.

You're already prescribing HEPs. clinIQ makes monitoring them billable.

sections.h2: Multi-patient visibility: 4 patients, 3 zones, zero guessing
sections.content:
Your therapist has Patient A on the bike, Patient B on the table, Patient C doing exercises with a tech, Patient D finishing e-stim. Four patients, three zones, constant context-switching.

When Patient A finishes the bike, how long until someone notices?

clinIQ Patient Flow does this:

• Zone tracking: Warm-up, With Therapist, Exercise, Modalities — where is everyone?
• Time in zone: Patient A in warm-up 12 minutes — alert, needs attention
• Therapist view: All my patients, all zones, at a glance — no hunting
• Tech coordination: Which patients need tech support? What exercises are prescribed?

No more patients sitting on the bike wondering if they've been forgotten. The dashboard shows everyone.

sections.h2: Visit cap visibility: No more surprise denials
sections.content:
Patient shows up for visit 19 of 20 authorized. Nobody realizes they're about to hit their cap. Visit 21 gets denied. You eat the cost or have an awkward conversation.

clinIQ shows this at check-in:

• 'Visit 18 of 20 authorized'
• 'Authorization expires: 4/15/2026'
• 'Re-auth needed before next visit'

Front desk sees it. Therapist sees it. Patient can be informed before the surprise.

No more denials discovered 30 days later. No more writing off visits you thought were covered.

sections.h2: Cancellation recovery: 90% of slots filled from waitlist
sections.content:
Patient cancels at 8am for a 9am slot. You have one hour to fill it. Your waitlist is in a notebook. By the time you work through it, the slot is gone.

clinIQ Waitlist does this:

• Automatic notification: Cancellation at 8:02am. Three waitlist patients get texts by 8:03am.
• First to confirm gets the slot: No phone tag, no chasing.
• Filled by 8:15am: Slot recovered, revenue captured.
• Waitlist analytics: How many slots recovered? How much revenue saved?

90% of cancellation slots filled. That's 4-5 recovered appointments per week. $400-500/week in revenue that would have been lost.

sections.h2: Flow efficiency = documentation time
sections.content:
Your therapists do 4-6 hours of unpaid documentation weekly. Notes after patients leave. Plans at lunch. Paperwork at home.

clinIQ doesn't do your documentation — that's your EHR. But smoother flow creates breathing room:

• Less time hunting for patients across zones
• Less time figuring out who's next
• Less time answering 'where's my therapist?'
• More time for documentation during the day

When your workflow runs smoother, documentation happens during work hours, not after.

sections.h2: Built for multi-patient PT, not single-provider templates
sections.content:
Most software assumes one provider, one patient, one room. That's not PT.

Week 1: We map your zones — warm-up, manual, exercise, modalities. We configure for concurrent patients, not sequential.

Week 2: Training. Therapists see their multi-patient view. Techs see their assignments. Front desk sees arrivals and caps.

Week 3: Go live. Zone tracking active. RTM capturing compliance. Waitlist filling cancellations.

Week 4: You're running. No patients forgotten. Revenue billing. Caps visible.

workflow.note: Patients may cycle through zones multiple times. Therapist sees all their patients across all zones simultaneously.

meta.title: Infusion Center Software | Chair Utilization & Drug Waste Prevention | clinIQ
meta.description: 15% higher chair utilization. Zero wasted biologics from no-shows. $7,200/month in RTM between infusions. clinIQ for infusion centers.

hero.h1: 15% more chair utilization. Zero wasted Remicade.
hero.subhead: Your revenue ceiling is chairs × hours × utilization. Right now you're at 70-75%. clinIQ gets you to 85%. That's 18 more chair-hours per day on a 12-chair unit. Plus RTM revenue between infusions.

sections.h2: Chair utilization: 15% increase = $300K+/year
sections.content:
12 chairs. 10 hours. 120 chair-hours of theoretical capacity per day. You're running at 70-75%. That's 30-36 chair-hours lost daily.

clinIQ shows you where utilization leaks:

• Scheduling gaps: 45-minute hole between a 2-hour infusion and a 3-hour infusion — visible, fillable
• Late starts: Patient checked in but chair not ready — why?
• Turnover delays: Chair empty 25 minutes between patients — where's the bottleneck?
• Real-time chair status: Occupied (time remaining), Available, Turnover, Blocked

The math: 12 chairs × 15% improvement × $150/chair-hour = $270/day = $67,500/year.

On infusion reimbursements, the number is much higher. One additional Remicade infusion per day = $4,000-6,000/day.

sections.h2: Drug waste prevention: $0 wasted biologics
sections.content:
Remicade is mixed when the patient checks in. Patient no-shows? That's $4,000 of biologic, wasted.

clinIQ syncs pharmacy prep with patient arrival:

• Patient checked in: Pharmacy sees it immediately — start prep
• Patient late: Pharmacy sees delay — hold prep
• Patient no-show: Drug not yet mixed — no waste
• Real-time arrival status: Not just scheduled time, actual arrival

No-show at 9am? You knew at 8:45 when they didn't confirm. Slot goes to waitlist. Drug never wasted.

One saved Remicade per month = $4,000. One saved Rituximab = $8,000+.

sections.h2: RTM between infusions: $120/patient/month
sections.content:
Your patients return every 4, 6, or 8 weeks. Between visits, they're managing symptoms. Reporting side effects. Calling with questions.

That's Remote Therapeutic Monitoring. CPT 98980 and 98981.

clinIQ RTM does this:

• Patient app: Symptom tracking, side effect reporting, medication adherence
• Nurse dashboard: Who reported issues? Who needs a call? Who's at risk before their next infusion?
• Automatic time tracking: Calls and reviews count toward 20-minute threshold
• Pre-infusion insight: Know how they've been doing before they sit down

The math: 60 infusion patients × $120/month = $7,200/month.

Your nurses are already fielding calls between infusions. Now those calls are billable.

sections.h2: Nursing load balance: No more 3 disconnects at once
sections.content:
Your nurse has 4 active infusions. Three finish at the same time. Now she's doing 3 disconnects while the fourth patient needs attention.

clinIQ shows infusion end times, not just start times:

• 'Chair 2 finishing in 8 minutes'
• 'Chair 4 finishing in 12 minutes'
• 'Chair 7 finishing in 15 minutes'
• Alert: '3 infusions ending within 10 minutes — Nurse B needs support'

Charge nurse sees the pileup coming. Rebalances before it happens. No patient waits for disconnect while the nurse is overwhelmed.

sections.h2: Prior auth visibility: No surprises at check-in
sections.content:
Remicade auth expired between appointments. Patient shows up expecting treatment. You're on the phone with insurance instead of starting the infusion.

clinIQ Pre-Auth does this:

• Auth status at check-in: 'Authorization valid through 4/15' or 'AUTH EXPIRED — DO NOT START'
• Expiration alerts: 'Auth expires in 7 days — patient not yet scheduled for renewal'
• Renewal tracking: Submitted, pending, approved, denied — for every patient
• No assumptions: Clear status before the needle goes in

No infusion started without auth confirmation. No claims denied 30 days later.

sections.h2: Built for infusion, not bolted on
sections.content:
Infusion centers aren't doctor's offices. Chair-based scheduling, drug prep timing, nursing ratios, reaction protocols — foundational, not features.

Week 1: Map your operation. Chair types, infusion durations, nursing model, pharmacy prep workflow.

Week 2: Training. Schedulers, nurses, charge nurse, pharmacy interface.

Week 3: Go live. Real-time chair status. Drug prep visibility. Nursing load balance.

Week 4: Utilization data accumulating. You see your baseline. You see the gaps. Optimization begins.

workflow.note: Adverse reactions trigger separate protocol. Drug prep happens in parallel, triggered by check-in status.

faqs.q: How do you prevent drug waste from no-shows?
faqs.a: Pharmacy sees real-time check-in status, not scheduled time. Patient late? Hold prep. No-show confirmed? Drug never mixed. Waitlist patient fills the slot.

faqs.q: How do you calculate chair utilization?
faqs.a: Occupied time ÷ available time. By chair, by day, by infusion type. Gaps and delays visible. You see exactly where utilization leaks.

faqs.q: Can nurses see when multiple infusions end at once?
faqs.a: Yes. Dashboard shows infusion end times, not just start times. Alerts when multiple infusions end within 10 minutes. Charge nurse can rebalance proactively.

faqs.q: How does auth tracking work for recurring infusions?
faqs.a: Each patient's auth status, expiration date, and renewal status visible. Alerts before expiration. No infusion started without confirmed authorization.

faqs.q: Is RTM between infusions actually billable?
faqs.a: Yes. CPT 98980 and 98981. Patient-reported symptoms reviewed by clinical staff, treatment decisions made between visits — that's RTM. Most payers cover it.

meta.title: Psychiatry Practice Software | No-Show Reduction & Private Check-In | clinIQ
meta.description: 18% fewer new patient no-shows. 100% private check-in. Real-time session visibility. clinIQ for psychiatry and mental health practices.

hero.h1: 25% of new patients no-show. That's $500/day walking away.
hero.subhead: New patient psych appointments bill $250-400. At 25% no-show rate, you're losing one new patient per day. clinIQ cuts no-shows by 18% with confirmation sequences and waitlist fills. Plus private check-in that respects why your patients are here.

sections.h2: No-show reduction: 18% fewer = $2,500+/month recovered
sections.content:
New patient no-shows in psychiatry run 20-30%. People make appointments during crisis. The crisis passes. The fear of the first visit remains. They don't show.

You can't eliminate no-shows. But you can predict and manage them.

clinIQ does this:

• Confirmation sequences: Text 3 days before, 1 day before, morning of. Each confirmation is a commitment.
• Non-response alerts: Patient didn't confirm? Flag visible. Call them or fill the slot.
• No-show risk scoring: Which patients confirmed every step vs which went silent?
• Waitlist fill: High-risk slot opens? Waitlist patient notified immediately.

The math: 4 new patients/week × 25% no-show = 1 lost slot. At $300, that's $1,200/month. Reduce no-shows by 18%? That's $200-300/month recovered just on new patients.

Plus follow-ups, med management, therapy sessions. The revenue adds up.

sections.h2: Session visibility: Know when you're behind before patients complain
sections.content:
Your 2pm was supposed to be 30 minutes. It's 2:45 and they're still in session — the patient disclosed something significant.

Your 2:30 has been waiting 15 minutes. Your 3pm just checked in. Your front desk is fielding 'how much longer?' questions they can't answer.

clinIQ does this:

• Session timer: Current session started at 2:00, running 45 minutes (scheduled 30)
• Wait time visibility: '2:30 patient waiting 18 minutes' — front desk can communicate proactively
• Provider queue view: See waiting patients on your screen without leaving the room
• Informed decisions: Continue because clinical need is more important, or wrap up — you decide, not by accident

No more discovering you're an hour behind when patients start complaining.

sections.h2: Private check-in: Dignity from the door
sections.content:
Your patients drove across town to avoid running into someone they know. They're anxious about being seen in a psychiatry waiting room. And your check-in process requires them to announce themselves at a desk within earshot of other patients.

clinIQ does this:

• Tablet check-in: Private corner, no verbal announcement
• Phone check-in: QR code from the parking lot — in your queue before they walk in
• Text when ready: 'We're ready for you, please come to Suite 200' — they walk straight to your office
• Queue display: Numbers only, never names, never reasons

Privacy isn't a feature. It's why your patients chose you over the hospital system.

sections.h2: Telehealth + in-person: One queue, one view
sections.content:
Half your patients might be telehealth now. Your check-in process needs to work for both without switching systems.

clinIQ does this:

• Unified queue: In-person and telehealth patients in the same view
• Telehealth status: 'John D., telehealth, in video waiting room 2 minutes'
• No system switching: One dashboard regardless of modality
• Same visibility: Know who's ready whether they're in your lobby or on video

Your telehealth patients aren't second-class citizens in your workflow.

sections.h2: Crisis flagging: Clinical staff notified immediately
sections.content:
Your front desk isn't clinical staff. They're not trained in de-escalation. But they're the first point of contact when a patient arrives in crisis.

clinIQ does this:

• Crisis flag at check-in: Front desk can mark 'patient in distress' with one tap
• Immediate notification: Clinical staff alerted before the situation escalates
• Care plan visibility: If this patient has crisis protocols, they're visible before check-in completes
• Documentation: What happened, who was notified, what intervention — clean record

Your front desk has a tool. Your clinical staff has visibility. Your patients get appropriate response.

sections.h2: Configured for mental health, not adapted from urgent care
sections.content:
Your practice isn't urgent care. The pace is different. The privacy requirements are different. We configure for mental health from day one.

Week 1: Map your flow. Individual sessions, med management, intake appointments, group therapy if you have it.

Week 2: Training. Front desk learns private check-in. Providers learn queue visibility.

Week 3: Live. Private check-in active. Queue numbers instead of names. Confirmation sequences running.

Week 4: No-show data accumulating. Session timing visible. Staff isn't fielding constant 'how much longer?' questions.

workflow.note: Simpler flow than procedural specialties. The complexity is in privacy and timing, not stages.

hero.h1: 8 minutes off turnover = $250,000/year in OR capacity.
hero.subhead: Your block time costs the same whether you do 5 cases or 7. Every minute between cases is margin you can't recover. clinIQ shows turnover in real-time, syncs pre-op with OR readiness, and tracks surgical prior auth with LOMN templates.

sections.h2: OR turnover: 8 minutes saved = one more case per day
sections.content:
You pay for 8 hours of block time whether you fill it or not. At $1,500/hour, that's $12,000/day.

Industry benchmark turnover: 25-35 minutes. High performers: 15-20 minutes.

Where's your time going?

clinIQ tracks every turnover:

• Patient out timestamp: 9:47am
• Room ready timestamp: 10:02am (15 min turnover)
• Next patient in timestamp: 10:11am (9 min delay — why?)
• Bottleneck identified: Next patient wasn't in pre-op

The math: 5 turnovers/day × 8 minutes saved = 40 minutes/day = 166 hours/year.

At $1,500/hour OR cost, that's $250,000 in recovered capacity. Or 1-2 additional cases per week.

sections.h2: Pre-op to OR sync: Zero cases lost to 'patient not ready'
sections.content:
Your OR is clean. Team is waiting. Where's the patient?

Still in pre-op. Consent not signed. H&P missing. Anesthesia hasn't seen them.

The OR team is standing around at $50/minute combined labor cost.

clinIQ syncs pre-op with OR:

• OR status visible to pre-op: 'Room 1 finishing in 15 min — Smith, Thomas next'
• Pre-op status visible to OR: 'Thomas in pre-op, consent ✓, H&P ✓, anesthesia pending'
• Priority alert: 'Room 1 ready, next patient not cleared — escalate now'
• Pre-op checklist: IV, consent, H&P, anesthesia eval — visible status for each

When the OR is ready, the patient is ready. No standing around.

sections.h2: PACU visibility: No more OR blocked waiting for beds
sections.content:
Surgery complete. No PACU bed. Patient stays in OR under anesthesia. Your room is blocked. Next case can't start.

That's not a PACU problem — it's an OR problem.

clinIQ shows end-to-end:

• PACU bed status: Available, occupied (time to discharge), blocked (waiting for floor)
• Discharge bottlenecks: 'Bay 3 — waiting for transport. Bay 5 — waiting for floor bed.'
• Bed availability prediction: 'Bay 1 expected open in 12 minutes'
• OR visibility into PACU: Know before you finish if there's a bed

You can intervene on discharge bottlenecks before they block your OR.

sections.h2: Surgical prior auth + LOMN: No case canceled for paperwork
sections.content:
Total hip scheduled for Thursday. Auth submitted two weeks ago. It's Monday — status 'in review.' Do you prep implants and hope? Delay and lose the slot?

clinIQ Pre-Auth does this:

• All pending auths sorted by surgery date
• Red flag: 'Surgery in 5 days, auth pending >10 days — escalate now'
• LOMN templates: Hip replacement, knee scope, rotator cuff, spine — auto-populated from patient chart
• Peer-to-peer tracking: Scheduled, completed, outcome logged

LOMN generation: Select procedure, select patient, generate letter. Prior imaging, conservative care, failed treatments — pulled from chart. Edit, sign, submit. 5 minutes instead of 30.

No case canceled because paperwork wasn't done.

sections.h2: Real-time surgical board: Everyone sees the same truth
sections.content:
Your whiteboard shows what someone thought was true an hour ago. Room 2 is listed as 'in case' but they finished 20 minutes ago.

clinIQ Surgical Board:

• Each room: Current case, status (prepping/in case/closing), estimated time remaining
• Next case: Patient name, procedure, pre-op status
• Turnover status: Room available, turning over, ready for next patient
• Auto-updating: No one maintains it. It maintains itself.

Charge nurse sees everything. OR director sees trends. Everyone sees the same board.

sections.h2: Live in your OR without disrupting surgery
sections.content:
We're not touching your surgical equipment. We're not interfacing with anesthesia machines. We're tracking patient status and room status — where people are, who knows, who needs to know.

Week 1: Map your flow. Pre-op, OR handoff, PACU phases, discharge.

Week 2: Training. Pre-op nurses, OR charge, PACU charge. Each role sees their view.

Week 3: Go live. Real-time surgical board. Pre-op/OR sync. PACU visibility.

Week 4: Turnover data accumulating. You see your baseline. You identify improvement targets.

sections.h2: Mohs efficiency: 25% faster stage-to-stage
sections.content:
Mohs isn't a procedure — it's a campaign. Patient arrives 7am. Stage 1. Lab processes. Wait. Clear? Done. Not clear? Stage 2. More waiting.

Where's the time going?

clinIQ tracks Mohs flow:

• Stage 1 complete — tissue in lab (timestamp)
• Lab processing — estimated 45 min remaining
• Results ready — margins positive — Stage 2 required
• Patient notified — returning to procedure room

Surgeon sees when results are ready without asking. Nurse knows which room to prep. Patient knows what's happening during the 90-minute wait.

The math: 6 Mohs patients × 25% faster stage transitions = 1-2 hours saved per Mohs day. That's capacity for one more patient or staff leaving on time.

sections.h2: Biopsy tracking: Zero results lost to follow-up gaps
sections.content:
Biopsy Tuesday. Path takes 5-7 days. Results come back: melanoma in situ.

Does the patient get called? By whom? When? Is it documented?

In a perfect world, there's a system. In reality, results arrive in a pile. Someone reviews them. Sometimes it takes a week. Sometimes results fall through the cracks.

With melanoma, 'fell through the cracks' is a malpractice case.

clinIQ Biopsy Tracking:

• Biopsy performed — pending path
• Results received — requires review
• Reviewed by Dr. X — patient contact required
• Patient contacted — follow-up scheduled for 3/15
• Documented: Called at 2:15pm, discussed findings, scheduled excision

Aging report: Any biopsy without patient contact flagged. No results sitting in a pile.

sections.h2: Cosmetic flow: Spa experience, not medical waiting room
sections.content:
Your Botox patient is paying $600 cash. They're not a patient — they're a client. They don't want to sit with sick people or fill out insurance forms.

clinIQ separates cosmetic flow:

• Private check-in: No forms visible to medical patients

Medical workflow handles insurance, vitals, provider visits. Cosmetic workflow handles consult, financial, treatment, aftercare, retail.

Same system. Different experiences.

sections.content:
Mohs suite, cosmetic treatment rooms, phototherapy room — each has different turnover requirements.

Your providers walk the hall looking for open rooms. Your MAs don't know which room to prep next.

Everyone sees the same board. No hunting. No double-booking.

sections.content:
Medical derm: Insurance, prior auths for biologics, chronic condition follow-ups.

Cosmetic derm: Cash pay, no auths, spa-level expectations.

Mohs: Multi-stage procedures, path lab coordination, variable duration.

clinIQ handles all three:

sections.h2: Same-day access: Better flow, not more slots
sections.content:
You promise same-day appointments. Those slots are gone by 9:30am. By 2pm, you're telling patients 'we can see you Thursday.'

The problem isn't slots. The problem is you're running 30 minutes behind by 10am. You can't absorb same-day patients when you're already underwater.

clinIQ Flow Visibility:

• Running behind alert: 'Dr. Smith 22 minutes behind schedule' — visible at 10am, not discovered at 2pm
• Same-day capacity: 'Dr. Smith has capacity for 1 more same-day. Dr. Jones: 0.'
• Proactive adjustment: Stop adding same-day patients when behind. Catch up. Then absorb.

Same-day access isn't a scheduling feature. It's a flow problem. Fix the flow, get the capacity.

sections.h2: Visit timing visibility: Know when 15 minutes becomes 25
sections.content:
Your schedule says '15-minute follow-up.' Your patient brought a list: blood pressure, back pain, depression screen, referral question, medication refill, and 'one more thing.'

Every 15-minute slot is a lottery ticket.

clinIQ Timing:

• Current visit timer: 'Patient with provider 18 minutes (scheduled 15)'
• Running-long alert: Triggers at threshold you set
• Pattern recognition: Which visit types consistently run long? Data for schedule template changes.

You can't fix visit complexity. But you can see it happening. Adjust in real-time instead of discovering you're an hour behind at 2pm.

sections.h2: AWV optimization: $175 in 30 minutes, not 45
sections.content:
Annual Wellness Visit: $175+ reimbursement. But 'while I'm here...' turns your 30-minute AWV into 45 minutes with zero additional revenue.

The problem: HRA done in the room. Screenings not routed. Provider doing data gathering instead of care planning.

clinIQ AWV Flow:

• HRA pre-visit: Completed via patient portal or arrival kiosk before appointment
• Screenings routed: Depression screen, fall risk, cognitive — routed to MA based on HRA results
• Provider focus: Patient arrives with HRA and screenings complete. Provider does care plan, not data entry.

30-minute AWV actually takes 30 minutes. Provider throughput improves. Same revenue, less time.

sections.h2: Built for the reality of primary care
sections.content:
We don't pretend your 15-minute slots are actually 15 minutes. We configure for primary care as it actually runs.

Week 1: Map your flow. Check-in, rooming, MA workflow, provider time, checkout. CCM-eligible patient identification.

Week 2: Training. MAs learn status updates. Providers learn queue view. Billing staff learn CCM/RTM documentation.

Week 3: Live. Flow visibility active. RTM enrollment starting. CCM time capture running.

Week 4: Revenue appearing. You see your baseline. You see where time goes. Optimization begins.

workflow.note: Simple flow. Complex timing. The value is visibility into how long each stage takes.

faqs.q: How does CCM time tracking work?
faqs.a: Staff logs care coordination activities — calls, refills, referrals, messages. Time accumulates toward 20-minute threshold. Monthly billing eligibility visible per patient.

faqs.q: Is chronic disease RTM billable in primary care?
faqs.a: Yes. CPT 98980/98981. Patient-reported BP, glucose, weight, symptoms — reviewed by clinical staff. Treatment decisions informed by data. Billable monthly.

faqs.q: How does this help same-day access?
faqs.a: You see when you're running behind. You stop adding same-day patients when underwater. You catch up. Same-day access is a flow problem, not a slot problem.

faqs.q: Can we optimize AWV throughput?
faqs.a: Yes. HRA before visit, screenings routed to MA, provider focuses on care plan. 30-minute AWV actually takes 30 minutes.

faqs.q: This seems simple. Why software?
faqs.a: The flow is simple. The timing isn't. Knowing you're 25 minutes behind at 10am lets you intervene before 2pm meltdown. Visibility is the value.
